definition_of_done.html.title: Definition of Done | Developer Docs
definition_of_done.html.summary: Definition of Done Tip For our 2-3 Developer Team: Efficiency is key. We rely on Automated Checks to be the primary gatekeepers. If the build fails, the tests fail, or the analyzer warnings persist, the task is not done. The Manual Checklist below is for the "Human Element"—verification of logic, design correctness, and user experience that simple scripts cannot catch. 1. Automated Checks (The Gatekeepers) Before you even open the PR, ensure these are Green. [ ] Build & CI [ ] Solution builds with Zero Warnings. [ ] All Tests Passing (Unit, Integration, Architecture/ArchUnitNET). [ ] 80% Code Coverage on Core Layers. [ ] Code Quality [ ] EditorConfig rules pass (naming, formatting). [ ] PR Title follows Conventional Commits (e.g., feat: add user notification). 2. Design Correctness (Decision Prompts) Verify using the prompts in Patterns and Principles: [ ] BDD: Did I write the Gherkin scenario before the code? [ ] DDD: Does each piece of code live in the correct layer? (Use the decision tree) [ ] CQS: Commands mutate, Queries read. No mixed concerns. 3. Implementation Correctness (Manual Checklist) Handlers & Logic [ ] Attributes Correctly Applied: [ ] [RequireEntitlement(EntitlementType.WordStandardTools)] (if feature requires license). [ ] [DocumentRequired] (if handler operates on an active document). [ ] Return Values: [ ] Return Result.Success() or Result<T> values. [ ] Do NOT return null or throw exceptions for control flow. [ ] If operation cannot continue, return Result.Failure. [ ] User Feedback: [ ] Use IUserNotifier for any user-facing messages. [ ] Must be Localized (no hardcoded strings in logic). COM Interop [ ] Resource Management: [ ] All COM objects released explicitly (using using blocks or Marshal.ReleaseComObject in finally). [ ] Two-dot rule followed (avoid obj.Prop.Prop chains). [ ] Layer Safety: [ ] No COM types leaked into Core/Domain layers. Configuration [ ] Settings: [ ] [Description] attribute added to all new settings props. [ ] Validation logic implemented (e.g., check paths exist at load time). [ ] Sample Configs: [ ] Debug/Sample configurations added to: C:\Brochet Dropbox\Brochet internal\Development\Word\Config Documentation [ ] Developer Docs: Updated docs/developer with "Why" not just "What". [ ] User Docs: Updated docs/user if feature is visible. [ ] Schemas: Validated (if changing config structure). Note Why this matters: As a small team, we don't have a dedicated QA department. This Definition of Done ensures that when we merge, we are confident the solution is stable, maintainable, and ready for deployment.

docs/Coding Reminders.html.title: Coding Reminders | Developer Docs
docs/Coding Reminders.html.summary: Coding Reminders Or things I forgot that seem silly when I was reminded Planning - Plan this out first - Even if you are implementing existing code - Think about what we are dealing with - Diagram the user experience - User stories Coding - Start with Tests - I have yet to see it work but I need to trust the process - Where is the code likely to go - Document - TemplateRepository - Domain or application - or wherever? - Does code exist already? Copilot's version of the above Coding Guidelines (BDD + DDD Aligned) Purpose A concise, copy-paste-ready guide for adding or modifying behavior using Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) and Domain-Driven Design (DDD) in this solution (Core.Domain, Core.Application, Infrastructure, AddIns). Core Principles • Let behavior drive design: Feature (Gherkin) → Failing Scenario → Minimal Code → Refactor. • The model reflects ubiquitous language; rename mercilessly when language evolves. • Keep business logic inside domain entities/value objects where it naturally fits. • One reason to change per class (SRP). • Prefer explicit over implicit; no “utility” dumping grounds. • Avoid premature abstractions; extract only after clear duplication. BDD Workflow (Always) Write/extend a .feature file (Reqnroll). Define Scenario(s) in Gherkin (Given/When/Then) using domain language. Generate only missing step definitions. Run: confirm scenario(s) fail (RED). Implement smallest domain/application change (GREEN). Refactor: improve names, enforce invariants, collapse duplication. Commit: feat(template): auto-attach default when opening untemplated document. Example: Feature: Auto attach default template Scenario: Attaching default template when enabled Given auto attach template is enabled And a document without a template is opened When the document open workflow runs Then the default template "Normal.dotm" is attached And the attachment is recorded in custom properties Architecture (Layer Responsibilities) | Layer | Purpose | Contains | Depends On | |-------|---------|----------|------------| | Core.Domain | Business model & invariants | Entities, Value Objects, Domain Events, Domain Services, Repository Interfaces | None | | Core.Application | Use cases & orchestration | Application Services, Commands/Queries, DTOs, Event Dispatch | Core.Domain | | Infrastructure.* | Technical implementations | Repos, persistence, integrations, file system, configuration | Core.Domain, Core.Application | | WordAddIn | Office entry point & UI adapters | Startup, DI composition, Ribbon/UI, OOM adapters | All (composition root) | | Tests | Specification & regression safety | Feature (.feature), Unit, Integration tests | All necessary | Dependency Rule: Flow inward only (UI → App → Domain). No reverse references. DDD Tactical Patterns Entities Identity-based (e.g., Document, Template). Enforce invariants internally. Expose intention-revealing methods (e.g., AttachTemplateIfMissing()). Value Objects Immutable; equality by all components. Replace primitive clusters (e.g., consider TemplateVersion, TemplatePath if rules grow). Aggregates Small, cohesive transactional boundary. One repository per aggregate root. External aggregates referenced by ID (not object graph). Domain Services Stateless, rare. Use only when logic spans multiple aggregates and does not belong to one. Domain Events Past tense (TemplateAttached). Immutable data; used to signal side-effects or consistency policies. Factories Encapsulate complex creation (validate before expose). Static Create acceptable for simple cases (already used in Template.Create). Code Placement Decision (Ask in Order) Is this a business rule or invariant? → Core.Domain. Is it orchestration/use-case flow? → Core.Application. Is it persistence, file system, OOM, configuration? → Infrastructure or AddIn adapter. Is it Office-specific interaction? → AddIn. Only test wiring/state? → Test project. If you’re modifying TemplateRepository: • Keep it pure infrastructure (materializing Template). • Do NOT embed business policy (e.g., when to attach). That belongs in an application service or domain method invoked by a use case. Naming & Language • Reflect domain terms precisely (no “Process”, “HandleData”, “Util”). • Methods narrate intent (e.g., SelectAttachableTemplates(), not GetList). • Centralize repeated constants (e.g., default template name) via configuration or value object. Office / VSTO Guidelines • Contain Word/Office Object Model (OOM) interactions inside AddIn/adapters. • Never pass COM types into Domain/Application layers. • Release COM objects deterministically. • Translate OOM failures to domain/application friendly outcomes (avoid leaking COMException outward). Error & Result Handling • Use Result pattern for expected failures (already using CSharpFunctionalExtensions). • Throw domain exceptions only for invariant violations—not control flow. • Never surface raw infrastructure/COM exception details beyond boundary. • Log with context but not sensitive data. Testing Strategy | Test Type | Focus | Examples | |-----------|-------|----------| | Feature (BDD) | Cross-layer behavior | Auto attach default template | | Unit (Domain) | Invariants & rules | Template naming/validation | | Unit (Application) | Orchestration | AttachTemplateOnOpen handler | | Integration | Repo/materialization | TemplateRepository parsing | | Smoke/AddIn | OOM wiring | Startup & DI boot correctness | Rules: • One assertion concept per test. • Assert outcomes (state/events), not implementation (no private field assumptions). • Step definitions stay thin: delegate to helpers/use cases. Refactoring Checklist (After Green) • Are invariants enforced at construction or mutator boundaries? • Any duplicated regex or filtering logic? Extract meaningfully. • Are public methods telling a story? • Any creeping primitive obsession? Promote to Value Object. • Any policy logic sitting in repositories/UI? Move inward. Anti-Patterns (Block Immediately) • Anemic Domain Model (no behavior on entities). • Putting conditional business logic into repositories (e.g., filtering decisions beyond raw retrieval). • Reusing mutable primitives for domain concepts (dates, IDs, versions). • “Utility” classes with mixed, unrelated methods. • Sprawling aggregates (too many responsibilities). • Tests asserting internal structure instead of observable behavior. Performance Notes • Only optimize hot paths after profiling (Debug > Performance Profiler). • Avoid unnecessary allocations in repository enumeration (current parallel bag usage is fine; reassess if contention appears). • Encapsulate regex compilation if reused heavily. Security & Robustness • Validate externally sourced input at Application boundary. • Do not log file paths or template contents if sensitive. • Enforce rule checking server-side (never trust UI alone). Commit Message Pattern Format: feat(template): auto attach default for untemplated documents fix(repository): ignore temporary Word lock files refactor(domain): introduce TemplateVersion value object Minimal Daily Flow Plan (Story + Scenarios) → Write/Fail Feature → Implement Domain/App → Pass → Refactor → Commit → Repeat. Boring, repeatable, safe. Quick Start (Template Example) Add scenario for selecting attachable templates. Run → fail. Ensure Template exposes any new rule (not repository). Keep TemplateRepository limited to discovery/parsing. Add application service if orchestration grows. Refactor names to match new ubiquitous language additions. Maintain this as a living document—update when domain language or architecture strategy evolves. Done. Let behavior lead design.

docs/DependencyInjection.html.title: Dependency Injection for the forgetful | Developer Docs
docs/DependencyInjection.html.summary: Dependency Injection for the forgetful Adding a command DependencyInjection.cs registers all Handlers that use ICommandHandler So when you run: var command = new AttachTemplateOnOpen.Command(); CommandDispatcher.Dispatch<AttachTemplateOnOpen.Command, Result>(command, CancellationToken.None); This is registered in DI and the handler is run Adding implementation The command handler may need to talk directly to the implementation, such as sending a message to the user Add the following to register the implementation with DI .AddSingleton<IUserNotifier, UserNotifier>() Domain Events Domain events are used to decouple side effects from command handling, allowing event handlers to respond to changes in application state independently of command execution. .AddSingleton<IDomainEventHandler<ToggleQuotesEvent>, ToggleQuotesEventhandler>() Registering DocumentEvents DocumentEvents must be registered with DI to manage its lifetime and ensure that event handlers are properly attached and detached from Word documents. .AddSingleton<DocumentEvents>()

docs/Localization-Guide.html.title: Localization Implementation Guide | Developer Docs
docs/Localization-Guide.html.summary: Localization Implementation Guide This document describes the localization infrastructure implemented for the Office Add-ins project, providing guidelines for maintaining and extending localized content. Overview The localization system is designed with the following principles: Simplicity: Easy to use and maintain Separation of Concerns: Domain errors separated from UI text Testability: Tests validate error codes, not localized messages Flexibility: Supports parameterized strings and multiple cultures Maintainability: Clear structure for shared vs application-specific resources Architecture Core Components ILocalizationService (Core.Application.Abstractions.Localization) Abstract interface for retrieving localized strings Supports parameterized strings and culture-specific lookups Keeps domain layer independent of localization infrastructure LocalizedDomainErrors (Core.Application.Common) Replaces static DomainErrors with localized versions Uses dependency injection to access localization service Maintains same API surface as original DomainErrors Resource Files Infrastructure.Common/Localization/DomainErrors.resx - Shared domain error messages WordAddIn/Localisation/Resources.resx - Word-specific UI text and dialog messages WordLocalizationService (WordAddIn.Common) Combines domain errors and UI resources First checks domain errors, then UI resources Provides unified localization for Word Add-in Resource File Structure Domain Errors (DomainErrors.resx) Contains error messages for all domain operations, using error codes as keys: <data name="Document.NotFound" xml:space="preserve"> <value>Document not found.</value> </data> <data name="Document.OpenDocumentError" xml:space="preserve"> <value>Error Opening Document with Id: {0}</value> </data> UI Resources (Resources.resx) Contains user interface text, dialogs, and Ribbon labels: <data name="UI.BrandTitle" xml:space="preserve"> <value>K+B Add-in</value> </data> <data name="UI.SearchRestOfDocument" xml:space="preserve"> <value>Do you want to search the rest of the document?</value> </data> <data name="Ribbon.Tab.KutanaBrochet" xml:space="preserve"> <value>K+B</value> </data> Usage Patterns Using Localized Domain Errors public class MyHandler : ICommandHandler<MyCommand, Result> { private readonly LocalizedDomainErrors _errors; public MyHandler(LocalizedDomainErrors errors) { _errors = errors; } public Result Handle(MyCommand command, CancellationToken cancellationToken) { // Simple error if (condition) { return Result.Failure(_errors.Document.NotFound); } // Parameterized error var error = _errors.Document.OpenDocumentError(documentId); return Result.Failure(error); } } Using UI Localization public class UserNotifier : IUserNotifier { private readonly ILocalizationService _localization; public UserNotifier(ILocalizationService localization) { _localization = localization; } private string BrandedTitle(string title) { if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(title)) { return _localization.GetString("UI.BrandTitle"); } return _localization.GetString("UI.BrandTitleWithContext", title); } } Dependency Injection Setup In ThisAddIn.cs: builder.Services .AddLocalization(options => options.ResourcesPath = "Localisation") .AddSingleton<ILocalizationService, WordLocalizationService>() .AddSingleton<LocalizedDomainErrors>() .AddSingleton<LocalizedErrorFactory>(); Testing Guidelines ✅ Correct: Test Error Codes [Test] public void Should_Return_Correct_Error_Code() { // Act var result = handler.Handle(command); // Assert Assert.True(result.IsFailure); Assert.Equal("Document.NotFound", result.Error.Code); } ❌ Incorrect: Test Error Messages [Test] public void Should_Return_Error_Message() // DON'T DO THIS { // Act var result = handler.Handle(command); // Assert Assert.Equal("Document not found.", result.Error.Message); // Brittle! } Testing Localization Services [Test] public void Should_Use_Localization_Service() { // Arrange var mockLocalization = Substitute.For<ILocalizationService>(); mockLocalization.GetString("Document.NotFound").Returns("Custom message"); // Act & Assert mockLocalization.Received(1).GetString("Document.NotFound"); } Adding New Localized Content 1. Domain Errors Add error code and message to DomainErrors.resx Add property to appropriate error class in LocalizedDomainErrors.cs Test using error code, not message 2. UI Text Add key-value pair to Resources.resx Update Resources.Designer.cs if needed Use through ILocalizationService.GetString() 3. Parameterized Strings Use {0}, {1}, etc. for parameters: <data name="UI.ReplacementComplete" xml:space="preserve"> <value>Replaced {0} items in {1} seconds</value> </data> var message = _localization.GetString("UI.ReplacementComplete", count, elapsed); Localization for Other Office Applications When adding Excel or PowerPoint add-ins: Create application-specific resource files (e.g., ExcelAddIn/Localisation/Resources.resx) Create application-specific localization service (e.g., ExcelLocalizationService) Register in dependency injection container Domain errors remain shared across all applications Culture and Language Support Current Support Default culture (English) Automatic fallback to key if resource not found Support for culture-specific localization through ILocalizationService Adding New Languages Create culture-specific resource files (e.g., Resources.fr-FR.resx) Translate all values, keeping keys identical .NET will automatically select appropriate culture based on user settings Encoding and Character Support Resource files use UTF-8 encoding Supports diacritics and special characters Automatic encoding handling by .NET resource system Best Practices Use Error Codes as Keys: This ensures consistency and makes mapping clear Fallback Strategy: Always return the key if localization fails Parameterized Strings: Use String.Format pattern for dynamic content Test Error Codes: Never test localized messages in unit tests Shared vs Specific: Keep domain errors shared, UI text application-specific Resource Organization: Group related resources with prefixes (UI., Ribbon., Dialog.) Maintenance Updating Messages Modify resource files (.resx) Update Designer files if adding new entries No code changes required for message text updates Adding Features Define error codes in domain layer Add localized messages to resource files Use through LocalizedDomainErrors or ILocalizationService Debugging If localization fails, the service returns the key as fallback Enable localization debugging through IStringLocalizer if needed Use culture-specific testing to verify correct resource loading Migration from Hardcoded Strings When updating existing code: Identify hardcoded strings Define appropriate resource keys Add to relevant resource file Update code to use localization service Update tests to validate error codes instead of messages This maintains backward compatibility while enabling localization.

docs/NetDocs.html.title: | Developer Docs
docs/NetDocs.html.summary: NetDocuments Event handling Calling the NetDocuments saveAs dialog via their ComAddin.Object means that it runs synchronously so need something like System.Windows.Forms.Application.DoEvents. Calling it via the Word Application.CommandBars.ExecuteMso "FileSaveAs" it runs asynchronously. Save as new or save as existing document sequenceDiagram participant u as User participant nd as NetDocuments participant w as Word u->>nd: Save Document nd->>nd: NdBeforeBrowseEvent Note over nd: Dialog shows and on exit nd->>nd: NdSaveDialogResultEvent - Result 0 nd->>nd: NdBeforeSaveEvent nd->>nd: NdEditProfileEvent w->>w: DocumentBeforeSave w->>nd: DocumentBeforeSave nd->>nd: NdBeforeSaveEvent Save as new or save as existing document with cancel sequenceDiagram participant u as User participant nd as NetDocuments participant w as Word u->>nd: Save Document nd->>nd: NdBeforeBrowseEvent Note over nd: Dialog shows and on exit nd->>nd: NdSaveDialogResultEvent - Result 1 Open sequenceDiagram participant u as User participant nd as NetDocuments participant w as Word u->>nd: Open Document w->>nd: DocumentOpen nd->>nd: ndDocumentReadyEvent w->>w: DocumentChange w->>w: DocumentOpen Close and save sequenceDiagram participant u as User participant nd as NetDocuments participant w as Word u->>nd: Close Document nd->>nd: NdUnsavedChangesResultEvent - Result 2 nd->>nd: NdBeforeSaveEvent nd->>nd: NdEditProfileEvent w->>w: DocumentBeforeSave w->>nd: DocumentBeforeSave nd->>nd: NdBeforeSaveEvent w->>w: DocumentBeforeClose w->>w: DocumentBeforeSave There are only 5 events Event Description NdAfterSaveEvent Occurs after ndOffice Save. NdBeforeBrowseEvent Occurs before Save As dialog appears Remarks Using this event you can possibly change default profile attributes for new documents to show on the Save As dialog. NdBeforeSaveEvent Occurs before ndOffice Save. NdDocumentReadyEvent Occurs when user document was opened and ready for processing NdEditProfileEvent Occurs before ND document content saved inside echo location. Remarks Using this event you can change profile attributes after dialogs and update document NdEditProfileResultEvent Occurs when user edits profile attributes in NdSetProfile event. (0 for OK 1 for cancel) NdSaveDialogResultEvent Occurs when user closes save dialog. (0 for OK 1 for cancel) NdUnsavedChangesResultEvent Occurs when prompt about unsaved changes is closed. (0 Cancel, 1 don't save)

docs/configuration.html.title: Configuration | Developer Docs
docs/configuration.html.summary: Configuration The Office Add-ins can be configured using various JSON configuration files. The location of the Configuration files is typically within the AppData Folders. Changing the Configuration Location There is an appsettings.json file that can be used to change the location of the configuration files. The file is located in the Installation folder of the Add-in, typically C:\Program Files (x86)\Kutana+Brochet\Word\. { "Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Information", } }, "ConfigurationLocations": { "Shared": "%appdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config", "User": "%localappdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config" } }

docs/scripts.html.title: Scripts - Developer Guide | Developer Docs
docs/scripts.html.summary: Scripts - Developer Guide Technical implementation guide for the scripting subsystem, covering architecture, domain model, configuration, and extension points. Architecture Overview The scripting subsystem implements a Command Executor pattern enabling users to define document formatting and editing scripts through JSON configuration. Scripts are composed of immutable command objects that are executed sequentially against Word documents via VSTO COM interop. Architectural Pattern Evolution The current architecture represents a significant refactoring from the original design (October 2025). See ADR: Refactor Scripting to Command Executor Pattern for the full decision context. Previous Architecture (deprecated): IScriptCommand.Execute() → IScriptExecutor → DocumentScriptExecutor (adapter) → IDocumentEditor → Word COM Current Architecture (dispatcher pattern with individual handlers): IScriptCommand (pure data) → IScriptCommandDispatcher.Execute() → Dynamic dispatch → IScriptCommandHandler<T> → Word COM Key Architectural Principles Pure Data Commands: IScriptCommand implementations are immutable records with no behaviour Dispatcher Pattern: IScriptCommandDispatcher routes commands to appropriate handlers via dynamic dispatch Individual Handlers: Each command type has its own IScriptCommandHandler<T> implementation Backend Agnostic: Design supports multiple backends (Word, Excel, PowerPoint, PDF) CQRS Integration: Scripts execute via CQRS handlers in the application layer Clean Architecture: Domain layer has no infrastructure dependencies Open/Closed Principle: New commands can be added without modifying the dispatcher Layer Structure Domain Layer (Core.Domain.Scripting) Pure domain logic with no external dependencies. Script Aggregate Root public class Script : AggregateRoot<string> { public string Name { get; } public string Description { get; } public IReadOnlyCollection<IScriptCommand> Commands { get; } } The Script aggregate is the root entity representing a collection of commands to execute. The Name serves as the aggregate ID. Invariants: Name must be unique within the repository Commands collection is immutable after construction Command order is preserved IScriptCommand Interface Marker interface for all script commands: public interface IScriptCommand { } All command implementations are immutable records representing pure data: public record ApplyFontFormatScriptCommand( FontStyle Style, FontFormattingRange Range) : IScriptCommand; public record DeleteCharactersScriptCommand( string Characters, CommandRange Range, CommandSubRange SubRange) : IScriptCommand; public record ApplyStyleScriptCommand( StyleFormatting Style, FormattingRange Range) : IScriptCommand; Design Rationale: Commands contain only data (what to do), whilst executors contain behaviour (how to do it). This separation enables: Serialisation for audit logging Batching for performance optimisation Transformation and composition Backend-agnostic command representation Available Commands Command Type Purpose Domain Model Location ApplyFontFormatScriptCommand Character formatting Fonts/ApplyFontFormatScriptCommand.cs ApplyParagraphFormatScriptCommand Paragraph formatting Paragraphs/ApplyParagraphFormatScriptCommand.cs ApplyStyleScriptCommand Style application Styles/ApplyStyleScriptCommand.cs ApplySelectionFormatScriptCommand Combined formatting Selection/SelectionFormattingCommand.cs InsertTextScriptCommand Text insertion Insertion/Text/InsertTextScriptCommand.cs InsertFieldScriptCommand Field insertion Insertion/Field/InsertFieldScriptCommand.cs DeleteCharactersScriptCommand Character deletion Deletion/DeleteCharactersScriptCommand.cs EnforceCaseScriptCommand Case conversion EnforceCasing/EnforceCaseScriptCommand.cs SetCustomPropertyScriptCommand Set document property Properties/SetCustomPropertyScriptCommand.cs DeleteCustomPropertyScriptCommand Delete document property Properties/DeleteCustomPropertyScriptCommand.cs RunMacroScriptCommand Execute VBA macros Macro/RunMacroScriptCommand.cs IScriptRepository Repository interface for script persistence: public interface IScriptRepository { IEnumerable<Script> GetScripts(); Maybe<Script> GetScript(string name); Maybe<bool> IsEnabled(string mappingId); Maybe<bool> IsVisible(string mappingId); } Separates script data (scripts collection) from UI metadata (mappings). Application Layer (Core.Application.Scripting) CQRS handlers orchestrating script operations. Query Handlers GetScripts: Retrieves all available scripts public class Handler(IScriptRepository repository) : IQueryHandler<Query, Result<IEnumerable<Script>>> { public Result<IEnumerable<Script>> Handle(Query query) => Result.Success(repository.GetScripts()); } GetScriptEnabled: Checks if a script is enabled public Result<Maybe<bool>> Handle(Query query) { var enabled = repository.IsEnabled(query.MappingId); return Result.Success(enabled); } GetScriptVisible: Checks if a script is visible in UI public Result<Maybe<bool>> Handle(Query query) { var visible = repository.IsVisible(query.MappingId); return Result.Success(visible); } Command Handler RunScript: Executes a script's commands sequentially public class Handler( IScriptRepository repository, IScriptCommandDispatcher dispatcher, ILogger<Handler> logger) : ICommandHandler<Command, Result> { public Result Handle(Command command) { var scriptResult = repository.GetScript(command.ScriptName); if (scriptResult.HasNoValue) return Result.Failure($"Script {command.ScriptName} not found"); var script = scriptResult.Value; foreach (var scriptCommand in script.Commands) { var result = dispatcher.Execute(scriptCommand); if (result.IsFailure) { logger.LogError("Command failed: {Error}", result.Error); return result; } } return Result.Success(); } } Key Behaviours: Sequential execution - stops on first failure Logging of command failures Transaction-like semantics (all or nothing) Cancellation token support (via extension) IScriptCommandDispatcher Interface Dispatcher interface for routing commands to handlers: public interface IScriptCommandDispatcher { Result Execute(IScriptCommand command); } Design Note: This interface abstracts the backend implementation (Word, Excel, etc.) from the application layer. The dispatcher uses runtime type resolution to invoke the correct IScriptCommandHandler<T> for each command type. IScriptCommandHandler Interface Generic handler interface for individual command types: public interface IScriptCommandHandler<in T> where T : IScriptCommand { Result Handle(T command); } Enables: Individual command handlers per type Dependency injection per handler Programmatic reuse of script commands Open/Closed Principle compliance Independent testing of each command type Infrastructure Layer Persistence (Core.Infrastructure.Persistence) ScriptRepository: Loads scripts from JSON configuration public class ScriptRepository( IOptionsMonitor<ScriptConfiguration> configuration, ILogger<ScriptRepository> logger) : IScriptRepository { public IEnumerable<Script> GetScripts() { var config = configuration.CurrentValue; return config.Scripts.Select(kvp => MapToScript(kvp.Key, kvp.Value)); } } Key Features: Hot-reloading via IOptionsMonitor<T> Bidirectional mapping (DTO ↔ Domain) JSON polymorphism for command types Configuration DTOs ScriptDto: Represents a script in JSON public class ScriptDto { public string Comment { get; set; } public IList<ScriptCommandDto> Commands { get; init; } } ScriptConfiguration: Root configuration object public class ScriptConfiguration { public IDictionary<string, ScriptDto> Scripts { get; init; } public IDictionary<string, ScriptMapping> Mappings { get; init; } } ScriptMapping: Controls UI behaviour public class ScriptMapping { public string Script { get; init; } // References a script name public string EnabledWhen { get; init; } // Rules expression public string VisibleWhen { get; init; } // Rules expression } Design Rationale: Separation of scripts (what) from mappings (when/where) enables: Multiple UI buttons for same script Context-dependent visibility/enablement Rules engine integration Command DTOs Base class using JSON polymorphism: [JsonPolymorphic] [JsonDerivedType(typeof(ApplyFontFormattingScriptCommandDto), "ApplyFontFormatting")] [JsonDerivedType(typeof(DeleteCharactersScriptCommandDto), "DeleteCharacters")] // ... other command types public abstract class ScriptCommandDto { [Required] public CommandRange CommandRange { get; set; } } Example concrete DTO: public class DeleteCharactersScriptCommandDto : ScriptCommandDto, IHasSubRange { [JsonRequired] public string Characters { get; set; } [JsonRequired] public CommandSubRange SubRange { get; set; } } DTO Mapping: ScriptRepository handles bidirectional mapping between DTOs and domain commands. Common Infrastructure (Core.Infrastructure.Common) ScriptCommandDispatcher: Dynamic dispatch implementation Located in Core.Infrastructure.Common.Messaging, this dispatcher uses runtime type resolution to route commands to their registered handlers: public class ScriptCommandDispatcher( IServiceProvider serviceProvider, ILogger<ScriptCommandDispatcher> logger) : IScriptCommandDispatcher { public Result Execute(IScriptCommand command) { if (command is null) throw new ArgumentNullException(nameof(command)); try { // Resolve handler type at runtime based on command's actual type var commandType = command.GetType(); var handlerType = typeof(IScriptCommandHandler<>).MakeGenericType(commandType); logger.LogDebug("Resolving handler {HandlerType} for command {CommandType}", handlerType.Name, commandType.Name); // Try to get handler from DI var handler = serviceProvider.GetService(handlerType); if (handler is null) { logger.LogWarning("No handler registered for script command type {CommandType}", commandType.Name); return Result.Failure($"No handler registered for {commandType.Name}"); } // Dynamic dispatch: CLR resolves to correct Handle(TCommand) method at runtime return ((dynamic)handler).Handle((dynamic)command); } catch (Exception ex) { logger.LogError(ex, "Error dispatching script command {CommandType}", command.GetType().Name); return Result.Failure($"Error dispatching command: {ex.Message}"); } } } Key Features: Generic type resolution at runtime Dynamic dispatch to strongly-typed handlers Automatic handler discovery via dependency injection No modification required when adding new command types VSTO Command Handlers (WordAddIn.Features.Scripting.Handlers) All script commands are implemented as individual handlers. Example handler: public class DeleteCharactersScriptCommandHandler( ITextReplacer textReplacer, ILogger<DeleteCharactersScriptCommandHandler> logger) : IScriptCommandHandler<DeleteCharactersScriptCommand> { private readonly Application _wordApp = Globals.ThisAddIn.Application; public Result Handle(DeleteCharactersScriptCommand command) { try { _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = false; _wordApp.System.Cursor = WdCursorType.wdCursorWait; // Implementation using Word COM interop var result = textReplacer.Replace( command.Characters, string.Empty, command.Range); return result; } catch (COMException ex) { logger.LogError(ex, "COM error deleting characters"); return Result.Failure($"Error: {ex.Message}"); } finally { _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = true; _wordApp.System.Cursor = WdCursorType.wdCursorNormal; } } } All Command Handlers (located in src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Features/Scripting/Handlers): ApplyFontFormatScriptCommandHandler ApplyParagraphFormatScriptCommandHandler ApplyStyleScriptCommandHandler ApplySelectionFormatScriptCommandHandler InsertTextScriptCommandHandler InsertFieldScriptCommandHandler DeleteCharactersScriptCommandHandler EnforceCaseScriptCommandHandler SetCustomPropertyScriptCommandHandler DeleteCustomPropertyScriptCommandHandler Handler Pattern Benefits: Independent testability Focused responsibilities Programmatic reuse via DI No modification of dispatcher for new commands Clean separation of concerns Configuration Format File Location Scripts are loaded from JSON files via .NET Configuration system: Shared: %appdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config/scripts.json User: %localappdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config/scripts.json Configuration locations are defined in appsettings.json: { "ConfigurationLocations": { "Shared": "%appdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config", "User": "%localappdata%/kutana+brochet/Word/Config" } } JSON Schema The complete JSON Schema is auto-generated from the C# DTOs using SchemaGenerator: Warning It looks like the sample you are looking for does not exist. Schema Generation: Run SchemaGenerator project to regenerate schemas when DTOs change. The schema provides: IntelliSense in JSON editors Compile-time validation of configuration files Documentation of available properties and their types Enum value validation Complete Configuration Example { "Scripts": { "FormatLegalDocument": { "Comment": "Applies standard legal document formatting", "Commands": [ { "$type": "ApplyStyle", "CommandRange": "AllParagraphs", "StyleName": "Legal Body Text" }, { "$type": "ApplyFontFormatting", "CommandRange": "Selection", "SubRange": "All", "Bold": 0, "Italic": 0, "Colour": "RGB(0,0,0)" }, { "$type": "DeleteCharacters", "CommandRange": "EntireDocument", "SubRange": "All", "Characters": " " } ] }, "RunLegacyMacro": { "Comment": "Executes a VBA macro from the attached template", "Commands": [ { "$type": "RunMacro", "Options": { "MacroName": "CustomModule.FormatDocument", "Parameters": ["Standard", true] } } ] } }, "Mappings": { "formatLegalDoc": { "Script": "FormatLegalDocument", "EnabledWhen": "Always", "VisibleWhen": "HasEntitlement('WordStandardTools')" }, "runMacro": { "Script": "RunLegacyMacro", "EnabledWhen": "Always", "VisibleWhen": "Always" } } } CommandRange Enumeration public enum CommandRange { Selection, EntireDocument, FirstParagraph, LastParagraph, AllParagraphs } CommandSubRange Enumeration public enum CommandSubRange { All, FirstWord, LastWord, AllWords, FirstSentence, LastSentence, AllSentences } Adding New Commands Step 1: Create Domain Command File: src/Core/Core.Domain/Scripting/{Category}/{CommandName}ScriptCommand.cs namespace KutanaBrochet.Core.Domain.Scripting.{Category}; public record {CommandName}ScriptCommand( {Type} {Property}, CommandRange Range, CommandSubRange SubRange) : IScriptCommand; Step 2: Create DTO File: src/Core/Infrastructure.Persistence/Configuration/Commands/{CommandName}ScriptCommandDto.cs public class {CommandName}ScriptCommandDto : ScriptCommandDto, IHasSubRange { [JsonRequired] public {Type} {Property} { get; set; } [JsonRequired] public CommandSubRange SubRange { get; set; } } Update ScriptCommand.cs to register the JSON type discriminator: [JsonDerivedType(typeof({CommandName}ScriptCommand), "{CommandName}")] Note: For commands without Range (like RunMacroScriptCommand, SetCustomPropertyScriptCommand), inherit directly from ScriptCommand and implement Execute() to delegate to IDocumentEditor. Step 3: Create Handler File: src/VSTO/WordAddIn/Features/Scripting/Handlers/{CommandName}Handler.cs public class {CommandName}Handler( {IDependency} dependency, ILogger<{CommandName}Handler> logger) : IScriptCommandHandler<{CommandName}ScriptCommand> { private readonly Application _wordApp = Globals.ThisAddIn.Application; public Result Handle({CommandName}ScriptCommand command) { try { _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = false; _wordApp.System.Cursor = WdCursorType.wdCursorWait; // Implementation return Result.Success(); } catch (COMException ex) { logger.LogError(ex, "COM error executing {CommandName}"); return Result.Failure($"Error: {ex.Message}"); } finally { _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = true; _wordApp.System.Cursor = WdCursorType.wdCursorNormal; } } } Step 4: Handler Auto-Registration Handlers are automatically registered via Scrutor in ThisAddIn.cs: builder.Services.Scan(s => s.FromAssemblyOf<WordScriptCommandHandlerBase>() .AddClasses(c => c.AssignableTo(typeof(IScriptCommandHandler<>)), publicOnly: false) .AsImplementedInterfaces() .WithTransientLifetime()); No manual registration required: Scrutor automatically discovers all classes implementing IScriptCommandHandler<T> Handlers are registered with transient lifetime The dispatcher will automatically find and invoke your new handler Step 5: Add DTO Mapping File: src/Core/Infrastructure.Persistence/ScriptRepository.cs Add mapping logic in MapToScript() method: private Script MapToScript(string name, ScriptDto dto) { var commands = dto.Commands.Select(cmd => cmd switch { {CommandName}ScriptCommandDto c => new {CommandName}ScriptCommand( c.{Property}, c.CommandRange, c.SubRange), // ... other command types }); return new Script(name, dto.Comment, commands.ToList()); } Step 6: Write BDD Tests Feature File: tests/Core.Application.Tests/Features/Scripts/{CommandName}.feature Feature: {Command Name} As a document author I want to {capability} So that I can {benefit} Scenario: {Scenario name} Given {precondition} When I execute the {command name} command Then the result should be successful And {expected outcome} Step Definitions: Implement the Given/When/Then steps. Testing Strategy Unit Tests Domain Tests: Verify script aggregate invariants Script name uniqueness Command order preservation Immutability of commands collection Application Tests: BDD scenarios for each command Command execution logic Error handling Integration with repository Infrastructure Tests: Configuration loading JSON deserialisation DTO to domain mapping Configuration hot-reloading Integration Tests VSTO Tests: Word COM interop Command execution against real Word documents COM exception handling Resource cleanup (Marshal.ReleaseComObject) Test Structure tests/ ├── Core.Application.Tests/ │ └── Features/ │ └── Scripts/ │ ├── GetScripts.feature │ ├── RunScript.feature │ ├── ApplyFontFormatting.feature │ └── ... ├── Core.Domain.Tests/ │ └── Scripting/ │ └── ScriptTests.cs └── WordAddIn.Tests/ └── Features/ └── Scripting/ └── WordScriptCommandExecutor.feature COM Safety and Error Handling Critical VSTO Practices 1. COM Exception Handling Always wrap Word API calls: try { // Word COM operations } catch (COMException ex) { logger.LogError(ex, "COM error"); return Result.Failure($"Error: {ex.Message}"); } 2. COM Object Cleanup Release COM objects explicitly: Sentences sentences = null; try { sentences = range.Sentences; // Use sentences } finally { if (sentences != null) Marshal.ReleaseComObject(sentences); } 3. Screen Updating and Cursor Management try { _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = false; _wordApp.System.Cursor = WdCursorType.wdCursorWait; // Operations } finally { _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = true; _wordApp.System.Cursor = WdCursorType.wdCursorNormal; } 4. Infinite Loop Prevention int iterations = 0; const int maxIterations = 10000; while (condition && iterations < maxIterations) { // Operation iterations++; } if (iterations >= maxIterations) logger.LogWarning("Maximum iterations reached"); Performance Considerations Optimisation Strategies 1. Batch Operations Process multiple paragraphs/words/sentences in a single operation rather than one-by-one. 2. Minimise Range Access Cache Range objects rather than repeatedly accessing Selection, Paragraphs, etc. 3. Screen Updating Disable during bulk operations: _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = false; try { /* operations */ } finally { _wordApp.ScreenUpdating = true; } 4. Command Ordering Apply styles before character formatting Combine formatting commands when possible Use ApplySelectionFormatting for multiple format changes Known Bottlenecks Paragraph iteration: O(n) for each paragraph access Word/Sentence enumeration: Expensive for large documents Find/Replace operations: Can be slow on entire document Extension Points Adding Backend Support To support Excel/PowerPoint/PDF: 1. Create Backend-Specific Handlers // Example: Excel handler for applying font formatting public class ExcelApplyFontFormatScriptCommandHandler( ILogger<ExcelApplyFontFormatScriptCommandHandler> logger) : IScriptCommandHandler<ApplyFontFormatScriptCommand> { private readonly Excel.Application _excelApp = Globals.ThisAddIn.Application; public Result Handle(ApplyFontFormatScriptCommand command) { // Excel-specific implementation } } 2. Handlers Auto-Register via Scrutor Set up Scrutor in your Excel/PowerPoint add-in's ThisAddIn.cs: builder.Services.Scan(s => s.FromExecutingAssembly() .AddClasses(c => c.AssignableTo(typeof(IScriptCommandHandler<>))) .AsImplementedInterfaces() .WithTransientLifetime()); 3. Dispatcher Works Automatically The same ScriptCommandDispatcher from Core.Infrastructure.Common works across all backends: Dispatcher routes commands to backend-specific handlers No changes needed to domain commands Backend handlers can share interfaces/base classes for common logic Custom Command Types For organisation-specific commands: 1. Extend IScriptCommand public record CustomFormatCommand( string CustomProperty, CommandRange Range) : IScriptCommand; 2. Create Handler public class CustomFormatHandler : IScriptCommandHandler<CustomFormatCommand> { public Result Handle(CustomFormatCommand command) { /* */ } } 3. Register JSON Type [JsonDerivedType(typeof(CustomFormatCommandDto), "CustomFormat")] Architecture Evolution The scripting subsystem has undergone two major refactorings to reach its current state: Phase 1 (October 2025): Refactored from the original multi-layered architecture to a Command Executor pattern with IScriptCommandExecutor using switch expression pattern matching. See ADR: Refactor Scripting to Command Executor Pattern. Phase 2 (October 2025): Migrated from monolithic executor to dispatcher pattern with individual handlers: ✅ Replaced IScriptCommandExecutor with IScriptCommandDispatcher ✅ Migrated all 8 command types to individual IScriptCommandHandler<T> implementations ✅ Removed switch expression entirely ✅ Implemented dynamic dispatch via runtime type resolution ✅ Auto-registration of handlers via Scrutor Result: The subsystem now follows the Open/Closed Principle - new commands can be added without modifying the dispatcher, and each command handler is independently testable and maintainable. References ADR: Refactor Scripting to Command Executor Pattern Script Command Migration Guide User Guide - End-user documentation Interpreter Pattern - Design pattern reference VSTO Programming Guide - Microsoft documentation

patterns_and_principles.html.title: Patterns and Principles | Developer Docs
patterns_and_principles.html.summary: Patterns and Principles Caution Adhere to Agreed Approaches Consistency is our primary driver for velocity. Do not change architectural approaches (e.g., swapping CQS for Services) without discussing it with the team first. Least Surprise > Clever Solutions. 1. Core Philosophy Least Surprise & Consistency We value code that looks boring. If you solve a problem in Module A, the solution in Module B should look identical. Tell-Don't-Ask Encapsulate logic within your Domain Models. Don't pull data out to process it; ask the object to do the work. ❌ Bad: Anemic Model var user = _repo.GetUser(id); if (user.IsActive && user.Expiry > DateTime.Now) { user.LastLogin = DateTime.Now; // Logic leaks into the service/handler _repo.Save(user); } ✅ Good: Rich Model var user = _repo.GetUser(id); var result = user.Login(); // Logic stays in the User entity if (result.IsSuccess) _repo.Save(user); 2. Architecture: Where Does This Code Belong? We follow a rigid dependency hierarchy. Use this decision tree: Is it a business rule or invariant? ├── Yes → Domain (Entity or Value Object) │ └── Does it need identity tracking? │ ├── Yes → Entity │ └── No → Value Object └── No → Continue... Is it orchestrating multiple domain objects? ├── Yes → Application (Command/Query Handler) └── No → Continue... Is it calling external systems (DB, API, files)? ├── Yes → Infrastructure └── No → Continue... Is it translating user input to commands? ├── Yes → Presentation (VSTO/Ribbon) └── No → Re-evaluate. Something's off. Layer Summary Layer Contains Dependencies Examples Domain Entities, Value Objects, Domain Events None EntitlementType, Template, Script Application Commands, Queries, Handlers, Interfaces Domain CreateDocument, GetScripts, IUserNotifier Infrastructure Implementations, DB, File System Application, Domain TemplateRepository, CommandDispatcher Presentation Ribbons, UI Application, Domain Thin dispatch only Quick Checks Domain has no dependencies → If you're adding a using for infrastructure, wrong layer Application orchestrates, doesn't decide → Business logic in handler = anemic domain smell Infrastructure implements interfaces → Defined in Application, implemented in Infrastructure Presentation is trivial → Just dispatch commands/queries, no logic Anti-patterns ❌ if (user.IsActive && user.HasPermission) in a handler → belongs in user.CanPerform() ❌ Repository interface in Infrastructure → belongs in Application ❌ COM objects in Application layer → stays in VSTO project 3. BDD: Before You Code Decision prompt: "Do I have a Gherkin scenario that describes what I'm building?" Answer Action No Write the scenario first. No exceptions. Yes, but vague Refine until Given/When/Then are concrete and testable Yes, specific Proceed to red → green → refactor Heuristics Scenario should be writable without knowing implementation If you can't write the scenario, you don't understand the requirement One scenario = one behavior (not one feature) Anti-patterns ❌ Writing tests after code "to get coverage" ❌ Scenarios that describe UI clicks, not behavior ❌ Multiple When clauses (that's multiple scenarios) 4. CQS: Command or Query? Decision prompt: "Does this operation change state?" Answer Type Returns Yes Command Result or Result<T> No Query Result<T> with data, no side effects Commands Can Read Internally From CreateDocument: [RequireEntitlement(EntitlementType.WordDocumentManagement)] [RequireEntitlement(EntitlementType.WordStandardTools)] public class Handler( ILogger<Handler> logger, ITemplateRepository templateRepository, // reads IDocumentManagementService documentManagementService, IDocumentRepository documentRepository, IUserNotifier userNotifier, // notifies IApplicationEventDispatcher applicationEventDispatcher) : ICommandHandler<Command> { public Result Handle(Command command, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default) { var result = templateRepository.GetByTag(command.Value); // internal read if (!result.HasValue) { userNotifier.Alert(error, "Template Error"); // notify on failure return Result.Failure(error); } var doc = documentManagementService.CreateDocument(result.Value); // mutate applicationEventDispatcher.DispatchAsync( new DocumentCreatedFromTemplateEvent(doc.Value, template)); // event return doc; } } Queries Stay Pure From GetScripts: public class Handler(IScriptRepository repository) : IQueryHandler<Query, IEnumerable<Script>> { public Result<IEnumerable<Script>> Handle(Query query, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default) { return Result.Success(repository.GetScripts()); // read only } } Rules Commands read what they need internally IUserNotifier in command handler keeps notification testable Queries have no side effects Use domain events for cross-aggregate coordination Anti-patterns ❌ Separate query dispatch just to read data for a command ❌ Query that writes audit logs → use decorator or event ❌ Notification logic in Ribbon → untestable 5. Design Principles (SOLID) Single Responsibility (SRP) Handlers should do one thing. ❌ Bad UpdateUserHandler: Validates input, checks DB, updates User, sends Email, logs to file. ✅ Good UpdateUserHandler: loads User, calls user.Update(), saves. Separately: UserUpdatedEvents trigger a SendEmailEventHandler. Open/Closed (OCP) Extend behavior without modifying existing code. ❌ Bad if (type == "Word") ExportWord(); else if (type == "PDF") ExportPdf(); // Modifying this method every time we add a format. ✅ Good // Inject IEnumerable<IExportStrategy> _strategies.First(s => s.Supports(type)).Export(); // Add new class PdfExportStrategy : IExportStrategy. No change to caller. Liskov Substitution (LSP) Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types. ❌ Bad public class ReadOnlyList : IList -> throws NotSupportedException on Add(). Why? code expecting an IList assumes it can Add. ✅ Good Use IEnumerable or IReadOnlyCollection if the list shouldn't be modified. 6. Coding Standards Naming Conventions Remove ambiguity. Commands: <Verb><Noun> → CreateDocument, RunScript, ActivateLicence Queries: Get<Noun> / Find<Noun> → GetScripts, GetLicence ❌ Bad DocumentManager (What does it do?) ProcessData (How?) ✅ Good ITemplateRepository SaveDocument Logging Log for the poor soul debugging this at 3 AM. ❌ Bad catch (Exception ex) { _logger.LogError("Error happened"); // What error? What context? } ✅ Good catch (Exception ex) { _logger.LogError(ex, "Failed to create document from template {TemplateName}", template.Name); } When to Log: Info: Significant business events ("Document created successfully"). Warning: Recoverable issues ("File locked, retrying"). Error: Exceptions/Failures that stop the flow. Commits Follow Conventional Commits. ❌ Bad "fixed bug" "updates" "wip" ✅ Good fix(templates): handle missing template gracefully feat(scripting): add custom property commands chore: update nuget packages 7. Specific Patterns Factories vs DI ❌ Bad Creating a factory just to call new. public class ServiceFactory { public Service Create() => new Service(); // Just register Service with DI! } ✅ Good Runtime decisions. public IExporter Create(string format) { return format switch { "PDF" => new PdfExporter(), "Word" => new WordExporter(), _ => throw new NotSupportedException() }; } Records vs Classes Type Use Case Rule Record DTOs, Messages, Config Use when data is the value. new Point(1,2) == new Point(1,2). Class Domain Entities Use when identity matters. Further Reading These sources informed our principles. Links may change; the principles above are authoritative for this project. Area Source BDD Gojko Adzic, Specification by Example (2011) Gherkin Cucumber Team, BDD with Cucumber - cucumber.io/docs/bdd DDD Vaughn Vernon, Domain-Driven Design Distilled (2016) DDD Tactical Eric Evans, Domain-Driven Design (2003) - "The Blue Book" CQS Martin Fowler, "Command Query Separation" - martinfowler.com/bliki Clean Architecture Robert C. Martin, Clean Architecture (2017)
